attributes.stem:
A 72-year-old woman presents to a rural General Practice clinic after being found walking around confused in her unheated home during a winter storm. She lives alone and has not been seen for two days. She has a history of osteoarthritis and depression. Her medications include sertraline and paracetamol. There were no signs of alcohol or substance use at her home. The nearest hospital is three hours away.

On examination, she appears mildly confused but cooperative. Her pulse rate is 58/min, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 14/min, SpO2 95% on room air, and core temperature 31.5°C. Her skin is cold and pale, and there are no signs of trauma or falls. She is not shivering. Her Glasgow Coma Scale score is 14 (E4 V4 M6).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management of hypothermia?
options: Hot water immersion whirlpool bath at 40 - 42°C
Rapid external rewarming with heated blankets
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with moderate hypothermia (core temperature 28-32°C) but is haemodynamically stable with only mild confusion. Hypothermia is a core body temperature under 35°C. Patients with mild hypothermia (core temperature 32 - 35°C) present alert and shivery, whereas patients with moderate hypothermia are drowsy and do not present with shivers typically. The priority is to initiate appropriate rewarming while monitoring for complications.

Rapid external rewarming with heated blankets is the most appropriate next step. The patient must be stable and has adequate peripheral circulation. The method should be combined with a warm environment and careful monitoring till 32°C, and then passive rewarming can be done slowly.

Hot water immersion bath would be dangerous in this setting as it can cause peripheral vasodilation leading to core temperature afterdrop and cardiovascular collapse. It also makes monitoring difficult and increases the risk of drowning in a confused patient. Rapid rewarming is appropriate in cases of frostbite but not appropriate when the core temperature is reduced.

Administration of warm oral fluids could be considered as an adjunct treatment, but should not be the primary rewarming strategy. Moreover, confusion may increase the risk of aspiration for this patient.

Passive rewarming with space blanket only would be insufficient for moderate hypothermia. While passive rewarming can be effective for mild hypothermia, this patient's core temperature of 31.5°C requires active rewarming measures.

Urgent ambulance transfer to hospital would be excessive for this stable patient with moderate hypothermia, especially considering the nearest hospital is three hours away. The long transfer time could delay essential rewarming. Hospital transfer would be indicated if there was severe hypothermia (<28°C) or signs of cardiovascular instability.

The management of hypothermia depends on severity. Regular reassessment during rewarming is essential to monitor for complications.
learningPoints: Active external rewarming should be initiated for moderate hypothermia (28-32°C) with warm blankets and a heated environment.
Passive rewarming alone is insufficient for moderate hypothermia (28-32°C).
Patients are at risk of arrhythmias during rewarming for hypothermia.
references: Chavala M, Gallardo M, Martinez I, Bayego E. Management of accidental hypothermia: A narrative review. Medicina Intensiva. 2019: 43 (9).  Available at: https://www.medintensiva.org/en-management-accidental-hypothermia-a-narrative-articulo-S2173572719302292

Perth's Children Hospital, Perth, Australia, Clinical Practice Guideline on Hypothermia, [Internet]. 2023. Available at:  https://pch.health.wa.gov.au/for-health-professionals/emergency-department-guidelines/hypothermia
